2 Connect Your AI API Key

Each user brings their own API key. This keeps your conversations private and costs separate.
▶ How to get a Claude API key
  1. Go to console.anthropic.com
  2. Create a free account (or sign in)
  3. Click "Get API Keys" in the left menu
  4. Click "Create Key" — name it "LandWorks"
  5. Copy the key (starts with sk-ant-)
  6. Add $5–20 credit under Billing to activate it

$20 credit ≈ 400–800 full analyses with Sport AI.

Get your key at platform.openai.com/api-keys. Keys start with sk-. Add credit under Billing → Usage limits.
Get your key at aistudio.google.com. Free tier available with generous rate limits.

Welcome to LandWorks

Sport AI is ready to help you analyze parcels, identify permits, build proformas, organize submittal packages, and prepare documents for PE review — all in one place.

To get started:
1. Enter a Wake County PIN in the search bar
2. Fill in your project details
3. Ask Sport AI anything about your project
4. Use the Submittal Folders tab to organize your package

💧 Pre-Development Stormwater Analysis

4-step SCS/TR-55 Curve Number method · NOAA Atlas 14 storms · Wake County, NC

Enter the existing site land cover. Use Fetch from NLCD to auto-populate from national satellite data, or estimate from the aerial view above. Percentages drive pre-development CN, runoff coefficient, and stormwater sizing.

Step 1 Summary — Pre-Development Conditions
Composite CN

Pre-dev Q — 1-yr (ac·in)

—
CN uses dominant HSG from Step 2 soils (defaults to HSG-C if not yet fetched). For 1/2/10/100-yr storm volumes see Step 4.

Fetch soil map units from NRCS to determine Hydrologic Soil Group (HSG) — the key TR-55 input. HSG controls runoff potential: Group A (sandy, infiltrates well) → D (clay, high runoff). Typical Wake County Piedmont soils are HSG B/C (Cecil, Wake, Vance series). Override HSGs in the table if field conditions differ.

Queries USDA NRCS Soil Data Access API using the parcel boundary

All depths editable — override with site-specific Atlas 14 values from NOAA PFDS. Fetch button reads parcel centroid coordinates and opens hdsc.nws.noaa.gov/pfds pre-loaded for the site.
Sub-basin guidance: <2 ac: 1 basin · 2–10 ac: 1–3 basins · 10–50 ac: 2–5 basins · >50 ac: delineate by watershed divide using topo/LiDAR. Separate areas with significantly different cover, soil group, or flow path direction.
Drainage Areas / Sub-Basins
Name Acres Imprv % Mgd Prv % Woods % Unmgd % Wetl % Bare % GrROW % HSG CN
Click "+ Add Area" to define drainage sub-basins
Cover percentages don't need to sum to 100% — unaccounted area treated as bare soil. HSG auto-fills from Step 2 dominant group. No limit on sub-basins.

📖 Comprehensive Plan & Design Standards Notes

🗺 Wake County — All Jurisdictions

Council meeting videos and rezoning case archives for all Wake County jurisdictions. Each acts independently — build separate datasets for each. Use Claude in Chrome to watch hearing videos and paste transcripts.

📍 Filtered to PIN municipality —

📊 Case Intelligence Database — 0 cases loaded

Real rezoning case outcomes from Wake County municipalities. The more cases you add, the more accurate your project evaluations become. Raleigh approves ~96% of cases that reach a vote — you need denied cases from Cary, Wake Forest, and others to build a useful classifier.

↩ Show All Cities
📊 About the Raleigh scoring — important context
Your Phase 1 dashboard shows 16% avg approval probability — but 24 of 25 voted cases were approved (96%). The ML classifier isn't miscalibrated about risk factors — it's miscalibrated about the base rate. It scores everything as risky because it has almost no denied cases to learn from, and its probability function starts at 50% rather than Raleigh's actual 96% base. The Project Evaluator here uses Claude's judgment with the base rate context baked in, which gives more realistic probabilities. To improve the ML classifier: get 15–20 denied cases from Cary and Wake Forest — that anchors the low end of the scale. The one Raleigh denial (Z-25-25, R-10→NX-3, 0.09 acres) is nearly identical to approved case Z-02-25 (0.07 acres, same zone change) — proving the decision was about hearing-room politics, not project specs.

Paste the contents of your scored_cases.csv file below (including the header row):

🎯 Project Evaluator — Approval Probability

Fill in your project details. Claude compares against the case database and estimates approval probability with specific risk factors and improvement actions. Works best with 20+ cases loaded.

Track individual council members' voting patterns and stated concerns from hearing transcripts. Build profiles over time to know who to meet before submitting — and what to say to each one.

🎯 Pre-Hearing Strategy — Who to Meet & What to Say

Generate a tailored political strategy based on who's on the council and what your project looks like. Identifies who to meet one-on-one before the hearing, what each member cares about, and how to frame your project to each of them.

🗂 Data Expansion — Add Cases by City

Click + Add Case to paste raw text from that city's portal — Claude extracts the structured fields in seconds. Red = 0 cases, yellow = needs more, green = healthy dataset. Focus on cities with denied cases to improve classifier accuracy.

📍 Showing:
💡 Fastest ways to build your dataset:
  1. Raleigh is already done — click "🌱 Load Raleigh 2025 Seed" to pre-load all 30 Phase 1 cases instantly
  2. Priority: Cary and Wake Forest — both have contested cases with actual denials; even 5 denied cases will dramatically improve scoring accuracy
  3. For Raleigh hearing transcripts: use Granicus + Claude in Chrome → switch modal to "🎬 Transcript" mode to capture council member profiles alongside the case data
  4. Cary portal: carync.gov rezoning cases — select all text from a case page and paste into "📋 Paste & Extract"

Full archive 2009–2024 of ASR, COA, Subdivision & Plat cases with stamped PDF links. Free, public record.

Grading, E&SC, building, and stormwater permits. Public records after July 1, 2018. Search by address, PIN, or owner. Includes uploaded plan sheets.
permitsearch.wake.gov ↗

Post-construction stormwater permits statewide. PE-sealed designs with BMP sizing and stage-storage. Search by project name, developer, or county.
edocs.deq.nc.gov ↗ NC DEQ Stormwater Program ↗

For any document not yet online: Wake County NextRequest and Raleigh records portal. Average 5–10 business day turnaround. Specific plan sheets, SWPPP, etc.

Parcel boundaries, zoning, floodplains, soils, watershed overlays, utilities, aerial imagery. Most comprehensive spatial data for Wake County.
maps.raleighnc.gov/iMAPS ↗

📋 Paste & Extract

Open a city planning portal, find a rezoning case page, select all the text, and paste it below. Claude extracts all structured fields and saves to the case database.

📐 Construction Drawing Intelligence — What reviewers check, municipality-specific requirements, design tips

Raleigh Administrative Site Review (ASR) — Required Plan Sheets

Applies to Tier Two and Tier Three site plans per UDO §10.2.8. Submittal via Permit and Development Portal. Sketch Plan Review strongly recommended before submission. Contact: 919-996-2500 | ds.help@raleighnc.gov

1. Cover SheetPlanning / Zoning
Project name, PINs, zoning, overlay districts, frontage type, existing/proposed uses, building SF, residential density, parking calculations, vicinity map (1"=500'–1000'), adopted zoning conditions
2. Existing ConditionsPlanning / Stormwater
Site size with metes & bounds, setbacks, existing structures, utilities & easements, topography, adjacent streets (names + R/W widths), sidewalks, water/sewer, built improvements, vegetation
3. Demolition PlanPlanning
Clearly indicate all items to be removed. Required if any demo occurs.
4. Proposed Site PlanPlanning / Transportation / Zoning / Greenways
North arrow & scale; distinguish existing vs. proposed; setback/build-to lines; streetscape; mechanical equipment (HVAC, generators); sidewalks/walkways/trails; solid waste facilities; parking + calculations (UDO §7.1.2); amenity area (UDO §1.5.3); open space/greenways; transition protective yards (UDO §7.2.4); site data table
5. Proposed Grading PlanStormwater / Watershed
Limits of land disturbance; grading; finished floor elevations; stream buffers (labeled); impervious surface calculations; tree protection fencing; retaining walls (top + bottom elevations); stormwater ponds/bioretention
6. Stormwater PlanStormwater / Utilities
Preliminary stormwater quantity & quality summary + calculations. If not required, provide note citing UDO section on cover sheet.
7. Utility PlanUtilities / Fire
All utilities shown underground; above-ground utilities with required screening (UDO §7.2.5.D); fire hydrant locations and access
8. Lighting PlanZoning / Planning
Pole-mounted fixture locations & heights (to top of fixture); building-mounted fixtures; compliance with UDO §7.4 photometric standards
9. Tree Conservation PlanForestry
Secondary tree conservation areas; tree cover report by certified arborist, NC licensed landscape architect, or NC registered forester. If not required, note it on cover sheet.
10. Landscape PlanForestry / Planning
Existing vegetation to remain; proposed landscaping with min. size & species mix; plant list; yard type labels; parking lot landscaping (UDO §7.1.7); existing/proposed parking lot light fixtures
11. Architectural ElevationsUrban Design / Comprehensive Planning
Building height per UDO §§1.5.7, 2.3, 3.2, 3.3; transparency per UDO §§1.5.9, 2.3, 3.2; blank wall area per UDO §1.5.10
⚠️ Mailed Notification Required when: New buildings >25,000 SF (or additions ≥10% of area or 25,000 SF) AND subject property is within 100 ft of R-1, R-2, R-4, R-6, or R-10 zoning. Mail stamped envelopes to Jeff Caines, Sr. Planner, One Exchange Plaza Suite 400, Raleigh NC 27601. Get property list from iMaps.

Apex Tree Protection & RCA Requirements

📍 RCA Requirements by Location
North/East of NC 540: ≥20% gross site acreage
South/West NC 540 (SF/TH): ≥30% gross site acreage
South/West NC 540 (MF/mixed): ≥25% gross site acreage
Inside Apex Peakway: ≥10% gross site acreage
Mass Grading (SF Detached): +5% additional
⚠️ Pools, tennis courts currently count as 50% RCA credit — this may be eliminated by upcoming UDO amendment
🌳 Current Tree Policies
Removal permit required: trees >8" caliper (SF/duplex exempt)
Native/adaptive species: ALL landscaping
Parking lot shading: 35% at maturity
Parking tree spacing: no point >40ft from large tree, >20ft from small
Replacement cost: $20/sf disturbed area (max $40,000) OR replace ≥8" caliper with ≥8" caliper
🚨 COMING CHANGES (EAB High Priority Recommendations — April 2026 Council Work Session):
1. Heritage/Champion Trees: Trees ≥24" DBH (all species) + Longleaf pines ≥18" DBH — must be preserved unless no feasible alternative. Replacement ratio: 2 caliper inches per inch removed. Currently only pine ≥18" caliper in RCA/buffers is protected.
2. RCA Reform: Restrict or eliminate impervious surface credit under §8.1.2.B.1.g. Pools, tennis courts, tot lots likely removed from RCA calculation.
3. Critical Root Zone (CRZ) Expansion: CRZ protection extended to ALL trees (not just those >8"). CRZ = 1.5 ft radius per inch of DBH. SRZ = 0.5 ft radius per inch of DBH. No grading, compaction, or trenching inside CRZ.
4. Landscape Standards: ≥75% native species required; ≤25% max per species/cultivar; 600 cu ft min soil volume for large canopy trees.
5. Tree Mitigation Fund: Unauthorized removal at double standard rate + stop-work orders for repeat violations.
✏️ Construction Drawing Tips for Apex Projects:
• Flag ALL trees >8" DBH on existing conditions sheet — field verify before submittal
• Show CRZ boundaries on grading plan: CRZ radius = 1.5 ft × DBH (inches), measured from trunk face
• Keep grading, equipment, stockpiling, and trenching OUTSIDE CRZ
• Design RCA to avoid pools/courts counting — assume this credit will be eliminated
• Landscape plan: use Apex Approved Plant List (Design & Development Manual); confirm ≥75% native
• Parking lots: design tree spacing so no space is >40ft from a large tree — reduces plan review comments
• If any tree ≥24" DBH exists on site, document preservation plan or mitigation before submitting

Raleigh ASR Review Team — Who Checks What

🏛
Planning
UDO compliance (setbacks, build-to, use, density), site data table accuracy, open space, amenity areas, transition protective yards, streetscape, Comprehensive Plan consistency
🚗
Transportation
Parking counts and dimensions (UDO §7.1.2), access and driveway locations, TIA requirements, pedestrian connections, bicycle infrastructure, transit access
🎨
Urban Design
Architectural elevations — building height, blank walls (UDO §1.5.10), transparency (UDO §1.5.9), facade articulation, street-facing design standards for specific overlay districts
🌊
Stormwater
Impervious surface calculations, BMP locations and sizing, stream buffer compliance, stormwater quantity and quality calculations, Jordan Lake / Neuse basin nitrogen/phosphorus loading
🔧
Public Utilities
Water/sewer alignment and sizing per Public Utilities Design Manual, meter locations, backflow prevention, fire flow requirements, connection points
🚒
Fire
Fire apparatus access road widths (minimum 20 ft clear), turning radii, hydrant locations and spacing, sprinkler system requirements for buildings >5,000 SF
📏
Zoning
Zoning district compliance, conditional use conditions, overlay district requirements, nonconformity issues, use-specific standards (UDO Chapter 6)
🌲
Forestry
Tree conservation plan completeness, tree survey by certified arborist/LA/forester, species identification, canopy calculations, tree protection fencing on grading plan
🚴
Greenways
Greenway easement dedication requirements, trail connections to adjacent greenway network, riparian buffer greenway dedication

Design for Cost, Time & Ease of Construction

💰 Cost Efficiency
• Minimize cut/fill imbalance — aim for balanced earthwork within site. Every truck off-site is $12–18/cy including haul and disposal.
• Avoid deep utilities — shallow water/sewer (8–10 ft) vs. deep (15+ ft) can double unit costs. Coordinate with utility authority on connection point elevation early.
• Standard pipe sizes — use 15", 18", 24" RCP for storm drain where possible. Non-standard sizes have longer lead times and premium pricing.
• Bioretention over wet ponds — wet ponds require permanent pool, riser structures, and forebay. Bioretention is simpler to construct and inspect.
• Cluster impervious areas — design parking and drives in one contiguous mass. Spread-out impervious creates more storm pipe length.
• Tree protection saves money — protecting an existing tree costs ~$500; replacing it is $1,500–5,000+ depending on caliper. Show TPF on grading plan.
⏱ Schedule Efficiency (avoid review delays)
• Pre-app meeting first — Raleigh Sketch Plan Review takes 2–4 weeks but saves 30–60 days on resubmittals. Worth it for projects >50 units or >10,000 SF.
• Stormwater calcs at submission — submitting "TBD" stormwater calculations guarantees a review comment. Run prelim numbers before day 1.
• Don't leave sheets blank — a missing lighting plan or landscape plan triggers a "hold" that counts against you even if everything else is fine.
• Wake County applications expire in 6 months if you don't respond to comments — restart clock with resubmittal, don't let it lapse.
• Respond to all comments — reviewers will reject partial responses. Address every numbered comment, even ones you disagree with. Explain why.
🏗 Constructability Best Practices
• Phase E&SC before mass grading — install perimeter silt fence, inlet protection, and construction entrance before breaking ground. Inspectors will flag violations on day 1.
• Grade away from buildings — minimum 5% slope for 10 ft from foundation. Positive drainage prevents call-backs and FHA/warranty issues.
• Access roads early — get your construction entrance and access drive stone base down before concrete trucks arrive. Prevents tracking violations and SUE damage.
• Retaining walls >4 ft need PE seal — design early, it's on the critical path. Keystone/Allan Block system walls are fastest to build and easiest to inspect.
• Utility coordination — call NC811 before any grading. Get utility conflicts resolved in design, not in the field at $150/hr contractor time.
• Bioretention underdrain — install observation well and cleanout at head of underdrain for inspection access. Reviewers check this at CO.
🔑 Top 10 Reasons Plans Get Kicked Back (Wake County / Raleigh)
  1. Stormwater calcs missing or incomplete
  2. Impervious surface totals don't add up to what's shown on plan
  3. Tree survey not by certified arborist/LA/forester
  4. Parking count below UDO minimum (or no calculation shown)
  5. No transition protective yard shown adjacent to residential zoning
  6. Architectural elevations missing or don't show transparency %
  7. Stream buffer shown incorrectly (measure from top of bank, not centerline)
  8. Fire apparatus access road too narrow or turning radius insufficient
  9. Landscape plan uses non-native species without justification
  10. Site data table on cover sheet doesn't match actual plan dimensions

Raleigh ASR Case Studies — Real Approved Projects

ASR-0036-2024 — 4020 Tryon Road Apartments CX-5-CU · Z-60-23
📐 Site
6.61 ac gross / 6.54 ac net
3 bldgs × 5 stories = 319,218 SF GFA
220 units = 33.3 units/acre

🚗 Parking
1.5 spaces/unit → 330 spaces (max allowed: 585)
30 ADA spaces (5 van)
12 short-term + 18 long-term bike
All surface, no structured
🌊 Stormwater
Existing imp: 0.76 ac (11%)
Proposed imp: 4.95 ac (74%)
Max allowed: 5.11 ac (77%)
BMPs: Underground detention (65'×45') + Sand filter (60'×50')
16" orifice @ 428.5' inv, 16' weir @ 434.5'
Nitrogen offset payment required
🔧 Utilities
Domestic water: 4" meter
Fire line: 6" DIP per building (NFPA 13)
Irrigation: 2" meter + 2" RPZ
Sewer: 8" SCH40 PVC @ 0.5–1.0% min
Backflow: 4" Febco LF860 RPZ
📏 Setbacks (CX-5)
Building from primary street: 5 ft
Parking from primary street: 10 ft
Build-to line: ≥50% (achieved 60%+)
PL frontage on Lake Dam Rd
🏗 Grading
FFE range: 442–457.5 ft (15.5 ft variation)
Bldg 1 FFE: 451.0 / Bldg 2: 454.0 / Bldg 3: 457.5
Knee walls <5 ft (no PE seal needed)
Slopes: ±2–2.5% paving, 3:1 cut/fill

138 trees (shade, understory, ornamental)
679 shrubs (12 species)
4 existing trees preserved
Amenity area: 30,072 SF (10% of site)
10 street trees along Lake Dam Rd

Max 250 units (220 proposed)
Min 13 units @ 80% AMI → 37 provided
PL frontage: Lake Dam Rd
I-40 buffer: 5 trees + 3 understory + 40 shrubs per 100 LF
Affordable covenant before 1st CO

ASR-0015-2024 — Mitchell Mill Apartments RX-3-PL-CU · Z-82-22
📐 Site
10.46 ac total · Lot 1: 3.63 ac · Lot 2: 5.35 ac
R/W dedication: 1.49 ac
8 apartment bldgs + clubhouse + pool
Conditions: max 24 DU within 500 ft of N boundary; max 36 DU all other bldgs; $20K affordable housing donation
🌊 Stormwater
BMPs: Wet Pond + Underground Detention Storm Filter
Rational Method pre/post analysis
5.1 ac to pond (3.9 ac imp), 2.6 ac to underground detention
Offsite bypass through existing 24" RCP

Bldg 1: 264.70 / Bldg 2: 264.60
Bldg 3: 265.60 / Bldg 4: 264.10
Bldg 5: 265.60 / Bldg 6: 266.20
Bldg 7: 273.30 / Bldg 8: 274.10
(9.5 ft variation across 8 buildings)
🌲 Tree Rules (Raleigh)
CRZ = DBH × 1.25 ft (not 1.5 ft — Raleigh standard)
Shade trees: min 3" cal, 10 ft tall for ROW
Understory: min 1.5" cal single-stem or 6 ft
Soil volume: 600 SF per street tree
2-year warranty after final inspection
Planting window: Oct 1–Apr 30
🔦 Lighting (Raleigh std)
Parking avg illumination: 1.1 fc
Parking max: 2.3 fc / min: 0.3 fc
Max/min ratio: 7.7:1 / Avg/min: 3.7:1
Type A: 150w LED 4000K (13,017 lm)
Type B: 70w LED 4000K (8,506 lm)

US 64/New Bern Ave: variable ROW
🌊 Stormwater
BMPs: 2 Wet Detention Basins (large-site PD strategy)
80 ft relief across site (170–250 ft elevations)
FEMA floodplain present
Greenway easement: 60 ft width (G-175)
🟢 Open Space
6 Outdoor Amenity Areas (OAA): 0.10–0.79 ac each
8 Open Space tracts: 0.33–3.08 ac each
SCM tracts double as open space credit
Cemetery: 0.81 ac (preserved)
🔥 Fire Access
Inside turning radius: 28 ft minimum
Min inner wheel radius: 25'-4"
Apparatus road: heavy duty asphalt/concrete
Load capacity: 90,000 LB minimum
Hydrant within 400 ft (NCFC 507.5.1)
📋 Plan Sheets
L0.00–L0.27 (Cover, Zoning, Site Data, Open Space, Phasing, Subdistrict, Recorded Maps)
C1.0 Existing/Demo · L2.00 Site Plan
L2.10 Lots/Easements · L2.20 Waste Collection
L2.30 Fire Access · L4.00 Landscape
C2.1 Utility · C3.1 Grading · C3.2 SW Calcs
A201–A219 Elevations (19 sheets)
Civil/LA: Bass Nixon Kennedy (Dan Gregory) · Developer: Bell Creek Capital, Charlotte · Arch: Havenn Homes, Atlanta
⚡ Key Engineering Benchmarks (from actual Raleigh approvals)
Density Benchmarks
• CX-5 urban multifamily: ~33 units/acre
• RX-3 medium density: ~8 bldgs / 10.5 ac
• PD with floodplain/buffers: ~25 ac total per project
Parking Ratios (approved)
• Mixed-income multifamily CX-5: 1.5 spaces/unit
• ADA: 30 spaces per 220 (14%) — exceeds code
• Bike short-term: 12 / Long-term: 18 per 220 units
Stormwater BMP Selection Guide
• Constrained urban site (<7 ac): Underground detention + sand filter
• Medium site with open space (8–12 ac): Wet pond + underground detention
• Large PD with natural features (>20 ac): 2 wet detention basins
• Always: nitrogen offset payment to Jordan/Neuse mitigation bank
Utility Sizing (220-unit complex)
• Domestic water: 4" meter
• Fire line per building: 6" DIP (NFPA 13)
• Sanitary sewer: 8" SCH40 PVC @ min 0.5%
• Irrigation: 2" meter + 2" RPZ backflow
Raleigh CRZ Formula
• CRZ radius = DBH (inches) × 1.25 ft
• (Apex uses 1.5 ft/inch — different standard)
• Soil volume: 600 SF per street tree
• Planting season: Oct 1–Apr 30 only
ASR Approval Timeline
• ASR approval → Site Permitting Review → Building Permit → CO
• 3-year deadline: obtain building permit from ASR date
• 4-year deadline: complete construction from 1st BP
• Stormwater surety: 125% of construction cost before CO
Affordable Housing (Z-conditions)
• Typical minimum: 13 units @ 80% AMI for 30 years
• Developers often exceed minimums (37 of 220 = 16.8%)
• Alternative: $20K lump-sum donation to city affordable housing fund
• Restrictive covenant required before 1st CO
Raleigh Lighting Standards
• Parking target: avg 1.1 fc, max 2.3 fc, min 0.3 fc
• Max/min ratio: 7.7:1 · Avg/min ratio: 3.7:1
• Roadway type IV 150w LED 4000K (13,017 lm) for area lights

🪨 Geotechnical Requirements — Wake County Piedmont

Wake County sits in the Carolina Slate Belt and Piedmont Upland. Expect saprolite (weathered granite) at 3–8 ft, sound rock at 10–25 ft. All soils are residual — no glacial deposits. Key references: NRCS Web Soil Survey, Wake County Soils Report, NCDEQ Sedimentation & Erosion Control Manual.

Wake County Soil Series
Cecil (CeB2/CeD2) — Most common upland soil. Sandy clay loam surface, red clay subsoil. Moderate permeability. Good for foundations after stripping topsoil.
Appling (ApB/ApC) — Sandy loam over sandy clay loam. Low shrink-swell. Good bearing capacity. Common on gentle slopes near ridgelines.
Vance (VaD) — Steeper slopes, erodible. Moderately well drained. Monitor closely during mass grading.
Chewacla (Ch) — Floodplain/wet areas. Poorly drained, high water table. Unsuitable for structural fill without undercut and replacement.
Wake (WaD) — Urban mixed fill. Variable. Always verify with borings if fill suspected.
Boring Program Guidelines
• Density: 1 boring per acre minimum; 1 per proposed building corner for townhomes/multi-family
• Depth: 15–20 ft, or rock refusal + 5 ft — whichever is deeper
• BMP borings: 2–3 per basin; confirm no rock within 4 ft of basin bottom; SHWT required for infiltration feasibility
• Pavement borings: 1 per intersection + 1 per 200 LF of road; depth 5–8 ft
• Standard tests: SPT blow counts every 2.5 ft, USCS/AASHTO classification, Atterberg limits on clay layers, grain size on fill materials
• Timing: Borings during due diligence (before rezoning); full report required for CD submittal
Compaction & Earthwork Specs
• Subgrade (roads/parking): 95% Standard Proctor (ASTM D698), top 12 in
• Structural fill (buildings): 98% Standard Proctor, all lifts ≤ 8 in loose
• Embankment fill: 95% Standard Proctor, lifts ≤ 12 in loose
• Pond embankment: 95% Standard Proctor; clay core if sand/silt used
• Unsuitable material: Organic soils (OM > 2%), debris, Chewacla series → undercut and replace or surcharge
• Shrinkage factors: Piedmont clay 20–25%; saprolite 10–15%. Rock excavation: blasting permits required where SPT > 100.
Rock Depth & Special Conditions
• Saprolite depth: 3–8 ft uplands; 1–3 ft ridge crests; deeper in valleys
• Sound rock (RQD > 50%): Usually 10–25 ft; check USGS bedrock geology maps
• Shallow rock risk: SE Wake (Triassic basin transition), Apex/Cary ridgelines — budget $8–15/cy rock vs. $2–4/cy soil
• Subsidence/old mines: Central Wake / Raleigh belt — check NCDMR mine permits database before drilling
• Expansive soils: Triassic zone near Garner/Clayton — run Atterberg limits if PI > 35, swell tests on subgrade samples
• Underground utilities: 811 locate 3 business days before drilling; hand-dig within 18 in of marks
Retaining Walls & Slopes
• Engineered wall required: Any wall > 4 ft height, or supporting structure/road/utility
• Common systems: Segmental block (Allan Block/Versa-Lok) to 10 ft; MSE wall to 30+ ft; poured concrete cantilever for tight sites
• Cut slopes: Max 2H:1V for Piedmont clay; 1.5H:1V for saprolite with erosion matting; 1H:1V for competent rock
• Fill slopes: Max 2H:1V; bench at 5 ft intervals for slopes > 20 ft tall
• Geogrid reinforcement: Required when fill height > 10 ft or slope > 2H:1V in fill
• Wake County grading plan: Must show all walls; walls on recorded plat if permanent and in common area
Stormwater Infiltration Feasibility
• SHWT requirement: > 2 ft below BMP bottom (≥ 3 ft preferred per NCDEQ BPDM)
• Field permeability (Ksat): Design Ksat = measured Ksat ÷ 2 (safety factor)
• Cecil/Appling soils: Ksat 0.5–2.0 in/hr — marginal; bioretention OK, subsurface infiltration risky
• Chewacla (floodplain): SHWT often < 1 ft — use wet pond or sand filter only
• Rock < 4 ft from BMP bottom: No infiltration — switch to lined bioretention or wet pond
• Required report sections: Boring logs, SHWT depths, Ksat results, infiltration feasibility conclusion per NCDEQ BPDM §16
💡 PE Tip — Cut/Fill Balance: Always run a mass haul diagram before finalizing grading design. Piedmont projects with 15+ ft of relief can self-balance if the designer alternates cuts (upland) with fills (low floodplain edges, pond embankments). Imported fill typically costs $18–28/cy delivered; exporting excess runs $10–18/cy tipping. A 10-acre site with 3 ft of net import adds $200k+ to construction cost. Target ± 5% balance on projects < 25 acres.

📅 Project Milestones

⚖️ Regulatory Review Timelines

Federal, state & local agency clock
🌊 NCDWR — Individual 401 Water Quality Certification
NC Division of Water Resources · Triggered when project impacts streams or wetlands ≥ 0.1 ac
55–60 days
typical clean approval
Day 1–30
Public Notice
Day 31–60
60-day review clock starts at submission. 30-day public notice sent to NCDWR Listserv — issuance cannot happen until notice period expires.
⚠ On Hold: If DWR issues a Request for Additional Information (RAI), the clock may re-start from zero when materials are received. Budget 60–90 additional days if on hold.
📋 Submittal: deq.nc.gov 401 Permitting
Start date:
Est. approval: —
Public notice closes: —
⚓ USACE — Section 404 Nationwide Permit (NWP)
US Army Corps of Engineers · Covers most wetland/stream fill impacts under NWP thresholds
45 days
+ 30-day EPA review
45-day review
EPA 30-day (CWA §121)
Issue
45-day USACE review cannot begin (and NWP cannot be issued) until USACE receives a copy of the 401 Water Quality Certification from NCDWR.
EPA Section 121.12: Once USACE receives the 401, EPA has up to 30 additional days to review per Clean Water Act Section 121.12.
⚠ Incomplete Application Notice: USACE may issue an IAN — clock re-starts on receipt of complete materials.
📋 Submittal: Wilmington District Regulatory
401 received by USACE:
Est. NWP issue: —
